Search results: 



--- SOURCE 1: Quantifying uncertainties of permafrost carbon–climate feedbacks - BG ---
URL: https://bg.copernicus.org/articles/14/3051/

SUMMARY:
The additional warming from the permafrost carbon feedback is between 0.2 and 12 % of the change in the global mean temperature (ΔT) by the year



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Permafrost carbon feedbacks threaten global climate goals - PNAS ---
URL: https://www.pnas.org/doi/10.1073/pnas.2100163118

SUMMARY:
Assuming we are on an overshoot pathway, permafrost carbon will increase the negative emissions required to bring global climate back down to



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 3: Permafrost carbon-climate feedbacks accelerate global warming ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3169129/

SUMMARY:
Permafrost soils contain enormous amounts of organic carbon, which could act as a positive feedback to global climate change due to enhanced respiration rates



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 4: The impact of the permafrost carbon feedback on global climate ---
URL: https://iopscience.iop.org/article/10.1088/1748-9326/9/8/085003

SUMMARY:
Degrading permafrost can alter ecosystems, damage infrastructure, and release enough carbon dioxide (CO2) and methane (CH4) to influence global climate.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------

### Abstract

Rapid Arctic environmental change affects the entire Earth system as thawing permafrost ecosystems release greenhouse gases to the atmosphere. Understanding how much permafrost carbon will be released, over what time frame, and what the relative emissions of carbon dioxide and methane will be is key for understanding the impact on global climate. In addition, the response of vegetation in a warming climate has the potential to offset at least some of the accelerating feedback to the climate from permafrost carbon. Temperature, organic carbon, and ground ice are key regulators for determining the impact

enriched_points.expanded_content: Significant data gaps exist in permafrost regions, hindering accurate assessments of carbon emissions and climate impacts, particularly in remote areas that lack comprehensive monitoring.
Modeling the complex feedback loops between thawing permafrost and climate change is challenging due to interactions among various factors, leading to uncertainties in predicting future scenarios.
Climate projections face uncertainties, particularly regarding the magnitude of greenhouse gas emissions from permafrost thaw, complicating efforts to achieve global climate targets.
Addressing permafrost carbon feedbacks requires interdisciplinary approaches, integrating climate science, ecology, and social sciences to develop comprehensive strategies for mitigation.
Engaging local communities is essential for effective research and policy-making on permafrost issues, ensuring that local knowledge and needs are incorporated into climate adaptation strategies.
